data.title: Putin says Russia will carry out a nuclear test if another nuclear power does so
Sector Update: Consumer
Corn Futures Rise as Traders Set Aside Uncertainty -- Daily Grain Highlights
data.description:
By Kirk Maltais


--Corn for December delivery rose 1.1% to $4.21 1/2 a bushel on the Chicago Board of Trade Thursday, with President Trump's assertions of another meeting with Chinese President Xi within the next four weeks.

--Wheat for December delivery rose 1% to $5.14 3/4 a bushel.

--Soybeans for November delivery rose 0.9% to $10.22 1/2 a bushel.


HIGHLIGHTS


Shrugging Off the Negative: CBOT grains appeared to ride residual momentum from President Trump's Truth Social post yesterday saying he'll meet with China's President Xi in 4 weeks. Outside of that, factors moving the market appear more negative. "Sort of strange action as most of the inputs are bearish but all markets trading higher today," said Joel Karlin of Ocean State Research--adding that many traders are reluctant to attach much hope to Trump's post. Multiple firms released their forecasts for U.S. crop yields and production, showing reductions from the USDA's September WASDE estimates for corn but still at record highs.

Risky Business: Rising geopolitical volatility in the Black Sea supported higher wheat futures. Russia is a primary supplier of wheat throughout the world, and Ukraine also has a sizable agricultural output, although that's been hindered by war in recent years. Lifting wheat today is a Russian strike hitting Ukrainian targets, while resumed help for Ukraine from the U.S. appears to make harder Ukrainian counterstrikes possible. "Black Sea risk is gradually escalating," said Andrey Sizov of SovEcon in a note. Comments from President Trump about moving "nuclear" submarines closer to Russia also ratcheted up the tension.

Pledging Support: Soybean futures were lower in premarket trading, before turning higher as the day progressed. In an appearance on CNBC, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent told interviewers that "you should expect some news on Tuesday on substantial support for the farmers." President Trump posted on Truth Social yesterday that he was working to support farmers struggling with China's absence from buying U.S. soybean exports, and also said that he would be meeting with Chinese President Xi on trade within the next four weeks.


INSIGHT


Case Sensitive: Analysts are watching for "how sensitive prices will be to news or comments surrounding Chinese demand, or lack thereof," said ADM Investor Service in a note. The firm said that without resumed Chinese purchasing, the USDA's current projections for soybean export sales are "optimistic." Other analysts note that China has made a lot of its normal export purchases already, with Brazil and other competitors.

Stubbornly Large: Research firm StoneX released new estimates regarding U.S. crop yields. The firm said that it now forecasts corn yields to drop from the USDA's previous estimate of 186.7 bushels an acre to 185.9 bushels an acre. StoneX also raised its outlook for soybean yields to 53.9 bushels an acre, up 0.4 bushels an acre from the USDA's last estimate. The ongoing government shutdown may delay the October WASDE report if it becomes an extended shutdown. "The bottom line though is that both of these yields keep the markets well supplied," said Arlan Suderman of StoneX in a note.


AHEAD


Note: The government shutdown may impact the timing of USDA reports and data releases.

--The USDA is scheduled to release its weekly Grain Export Inspections report at 11 a.m. ET Monday.

--The USDA is scheduled to release its weekly Crop Progress report at 4 p.m. ET Monday.


Write to Kirk Maltais at kirk.maltais@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

10-02-25 1511ET

RPM International Inc. Declares Regular Quarterly Cash Dividend, Payable on October 31, 2025
data.description: RPM International Inc. announced at its annual meeting of stockholders that its board of directors declared a regular quarterly cash dividend of $0.54 per share, payable on October 31, 2025, to stockholders of record as of October 20, 2025. This payment represents a 5.9% increase over the $0.51 quarterly cash dividend paid at this time last year and is the 52nd consecutive year the company has increased its cash dividend.

San Diego to Issue $670 Million in Bonds to Back School, Housing Projects
data.description:
By Paulo Trevisani


The San Diego Unified School District plans to issue $670 million in general obligation bonds to fund investment in schools and housing.

The funds will finance projects including renovating and building new classrooms, according to documents posted Wednesday on MuniOs. The proceeds will also support affordable employee housing.

Proceeds will also be used for technology upgrades as well as for a range of projects that include repairing leaky roofs and plumbing to removing hazardous substances and improving classroom security, among other things.

The issuance is separated into three major series: Series P totals $100 million; Series I totals $250 million and Series C totals $320 million. Each series includes taxable and non-taxable tranches. The securities mature between 2026 and 2055.

Coupon and yield rates weren't specified. Pricing is set to take place on Oct. 7 for retail and Oct. 8 for institutional orders. Closing is preliminarily scheduled for Oct. 22.

The bonds are backed by property taxes.

Fitch has assigned a "AAA" ratings for most tranches, and a short-term F1+ rating to the Series P-1, I-1 and C-1 bonds. Moody's has rated the bonds Aa2.

Senior managers on the offering are Jefferies and Stifel, Nicolaus & Company.


Write to Paulo Trevisani at paulo.trevisani@wsj.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

10-02-25 1341ET

data.title: Senators call on Boeing to end eight-week-long St. Louis strike
data.description:
(Reuters) -U.S. Senator Bernie Sanders urged Boeing in a letter sent on Wednesday to end the eight-week-long strike in the St. Louis area, where it produces fighters, munitions and other military aircraft.

"Do the right thing," Sanders said in his letter to Boeing CEO Kelly Ortberg.

The letter was sent the same day Sanders and other senators used a Senate committee nomination review hearing to slam Boeing's behavior during the strike. 

About 3,200 members of the International Association of Machinists and Aerospace Workers District 837 went on strike on August 4.  

(Reporting by Dan Catchpole in Seattle, Editing by Chris Reese)
